name: RRR-SMR — Reduce, Reuse, Recycle
summary: Nikolaev, Boyle et al. (PLDI 2025): existing SMR schemes only allow reclamation (return to allocator), not reuse (in-place rebirth as a different node of the same type). RRR adds generation tags and tombstone discipline so retired nodes can be safely recycled in-place under HE/IBR/Hyaline-class SMR. Outperforms classical SMR-based data structures on contention-heavy workloads where allocation pressure dominates.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Nikolaev, Boyle et al.'s **RRR-SMR** ("Reduce, Reuse, Recycle: Better Methods for Practical Lock-Free Data Structures", PLDI 2025) addresses a fundamental limitation of every prior SMR scheme in this catalogue (`data/hazard-pointers`, `data/rcu`, `data/qsbr`, `data/epoch-based-reclamation`, `data/hazard-eras`, `data/interval-based-reclamation`, `data/wait-free-eras`, `data/hyaline-reclamation`, `data/stamp-it`, `data/vbr-reclamation`, `data/nbr-reclamation`, `data/folly-hazptr`): existing safe-memory-reclamation methods only allow *reclamation* of retired memory (return to the allocator), not *reuse* (in-place rebirth as a different node of the same type within the data structure).

Reuse is dramatically more efficient than reclaim-and-reallocate because it avoids allocator round-trips, but unsafe under naive SMR — a stale reader holding a pointer to the old value would see the new value and break invariants.

sections.title: Generation tags and tombstone discipline
sections.content:
The RRR contribution is a general method of **constructing data structures whose nodes can be safely recycled** under SMR by reasoning about which fields must be left untouched while a stale reader could observe them.

The mechanism uses *generation tags* in node headers that retired nodes carry into reuse, plus a discipline ensuring reuses are visible to stale readers as inert *tombstone* states rather than as inconsistent live nodes. Combined with HE/IBR/Hyaline-class SMR schemes (`data/hazard-eras`, `data/interval-based-reclamation`, `data/hyaline-reclamation`), RRR-recycled data structures outperform classical SMR-based ones on contention-heavy workloads where allocation pressure dominates.

The lesson generalises: **SMR research has converged on bounded retention; the next frontier is bounded reuse-vs-reclaim cycles**. For language designers building lock-free data structures into a language runtime (channels, lock-free queues, work-stealing deques), RRR-SMR is the current best technique for combining safe reader-side discipline with allocation-pressure-friendly reuse.
